stimulus: “Ultra-short-period planets” are rocky planets that have a mass less than 2 Earths and an orbital period of less than 10 days, while “long-period rocky planets” have the same mass criterion but orbital periods longer than 10 days. In 2022, astrobiologist Hannah Diamond-Lowe and her team reported the characteristics of five newly characterized rocky exoplanets and asserted that three are ultra-short-period planets and one is a long-period rocky planet.
stem: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Diamond-Lowe and her team’s assertion?

answerOptions.content: Three of the planets have masses below 2 Earths and orbital periods under 10 days, and TOI-700 d has a mass below 2 Earths and an orbital period over 10 days.
None of the planets have an orbital period of more than 10 days, and LHS 1140 b has a mass of 6.98 Earths.
All the planets have a radius between 0.918 and 1.640 Earths, and only TOI-700 d has an orbital period of more than 10 days.
TOI-700 d has an orbital period of 100 days, and the masses of all the planets range from 0.69 to 6.98 Earths.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because the team’s assertion requires three ultra-short-period planets (mass <2 Earths, orbital period <10 days) and one long-period rocky planet (mass <2 Earths, orbital period >10 days). The table shows LP 791-18 d, GJ 1132 b, and TRAPPIST-1 e all meet ultra-short-period criteria; TOI-700 d meets long-period criteria. Choice B is incorrect because TOI-700 d and LHS 1140 b both have periods over 10 days. Choice C is incorrect because radius is not a criterion in the assertion, and TOI-700 d is not the only planet with a period over 10 days. Choice D is incorrect because TOI-700 d has a 37.42-day period, not 100 days.
